policy_statement: WWTP operators will plan to install methane recovery systems in all Shelby County facilities by the end of 2035, with feasibility evaluation beginning in 2020 and an implementation and monitoring plan launched if feasible.
mechanism_description: WWTP operators plan to evaluate and, if feasible, install methane recovery systems (or biogas flaring) at wastewater facilities to cut methane released from treatment processes.
primary_causal_pathway: Wastewater treatment operations → CH₄ capture/flare (↓ fugitive methane) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: WWTPs add or upgrade anaerobic digester gas handling—covers, piping, compressors, gas storage, controls—to capture biogas and route it to beneficial use (e.g., CHP/boilers) or to enclosed flares rather than venting/leaking.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Methane generated during anaerobic wastewater/sludge treatment is intercepted; combustion converts CH₄ to CO₂ and H₂O (and/or prevents uncontrolled venting), greatly lowering climate impact due to CH₄’s much higher warming potential than CO₂ over typical policy horizons.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in methane emissions from the facility (and potentially additional CO₂ reductions if recovered biogas displaces fossil fuel energy, though that is secondary to the methane-control effect).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding high–global-warming-potential methane emissions from wastewater treatment by capturing and combusting/controlling biogas.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: control/destruction of CH₄ produced at WWTPs → reduced direct GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While energy recovery can improve resource efficiency and reduce CO₂ via fuel displacement, the mechanism as stated (methane recovery or flaring) is fundamentally about reducing methane releases; that is a direct mitigation pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a project only improves energy efficiency without addressing methane leakage, it would shift toward resource efficiency; however, “methane recovery systems (or biogas flaring)” inherently targets methane emissions control. Also, if combustion CO₂ is counted, it is still far lower climate impact than uncontrolled CH₄.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy implementing methane capture, control, or flaring at wastewater facilities shares the same primary effect—reduced fugitive methane emissions—so Mitigation is consistent across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy is best subclassed as “methane capture,” “renewable gas/CHP,” or “process controls/LDAR” depends on policy-specific details (use vs flare, monitoring rigor, displacement claims) and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a policy only installs CHP for energy savings without ensuring high capture/destruction efficiency (or allows significant upstream leaks), the dominant benefit could shift toward energy/resource efficiency; likewise, if digestion is newly introduced (increasing biogas generation) the net effect depends on baseline practice, but the canonical mechanism here assumes capture/flare reduces fugitive CH₄ versus baseline.
key_indicators: “plan to install methane recovery systems”; “by the end of 2035”; “Starting in 2020, staff can evaluate whether methane recovery or biogas flaring is feasible”; “launch an implementation and monitoring plan to achieve this goal”; “support from DPD and City government personnel”
co_benefits: Potential renewable energy generation/energy cost savings from biogas use; reduced odors/air pollutants from uncontrolled gas releases; improved operational safety (all not explicit enough to classify as separate categories).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a target/goal (“by the end of 2035”) or as an infrastructure investment, but the excerpt emphasizes feasibility evaluation and planning rather than a binding requirement or committed capital spending; therefore classified as a planning-strategy.
